content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 27, 2006


James V. Lacy
Wewer & Lacy LLP
Civic Center Plaza
300111 Ivy Glenn Drive, Ste. 223
Laguna Niguel, CA 92677


RE: Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-06-140

Dear Mr. Lacy:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of two slate mail
organizations regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

     If payments by slate mailer organizations for electronic communications that
support or oppose candidates and/or ballot measures are reported on the usual slate mailer
reports (Form 401) pursuant to Government Code sections 84218 and 84219, is it true
that no additional campaign reports will be required under sections 84200 or 84200.5?

CONCLUSION

     Yes, section 82048.4 expressly provides that contributions or expenditures by a
slate mailer organization ("SMO"), associated with the production or distribution of
slate mailers, give rise to no additional reporting obligations by the SMO under section
84200 or 84200.5, so long as those payments are reported pursuant to sections 84218 and 84219.

FACTS

     You are counsel to and treasurer of the "Save Proposition 13 Segregated Fund
Account," a slate mailer organization. You are also counsel to another slate mailer

_______________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109 -
18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-06-140
Page No. 2

organization, "Citizens for Representative Government." You are authorized to make
this request on behalf of these organizations.

On March 16, 2006, we issued the Lacy Advice Letter, No. A-06-017. Your
current letter presents additional facts and seeks clarification on a point touched on in our
prior advice letter to you.

The facts underlying the present request for advice are those found in Advice
Letter No. A-06-017, except that the slate mailer organizations ("SMOs") concerned are
now the two listed above. As we understand matters, then, these two SMOs wish to
extend their activities for the November 2006 election beyond traditional slate mail,
offering their advertisers the option, with additional payment, to appear in targeted
email communications sent by the SMOs, and on the organizations' websites. The SMOs
wish to offer this additional electronic distribution as an incentive for campaigns and
measures to advertise on their slate mailers. The SMOs will not seek or accept payments
specifically for these electronic communications, rather, they would be offered at no
additional charge to the candidates and ballot measures who advertise on mailings, as a
"bonus" for participating in the slate mailer programs.

For example, the Save Proposition 13 Segregated Fund Account seeks not only to
mail its traditional slate mailer in the November 2006 election, but also to send a series of
three or more emails to thousands of voters statewide, in support of candidates and
measures who have made payments to appear on slate mailers, as communications
supplementary to the slate mailer. These electronic communications may include support
or opposition to four or more candidates and measures in the text, but some may include
support or opposition to only one candidate or measure. However, all of the candidates
or measures will have made a payment to advertise in the slate mailer only.

The SMOs believe that if their payments for these electronic measures
reported on the usual slate mailer reports (Form 401) pursuant to Government Code
sections 84218 and 84219, then no additional campaign reports will be required under
sections 84200 or 84200.5. You ask for confirmation of this proposition.

ANALYSIS

As we explained in the Lacy Advice Letter, No. A-06-017, the Act provides that
political ads which do not fit the Act's definition of "slate mailers" do not enjoy the
special exemption of section 82048.4(c), so that "when an SMO engages in the
production and dissemination of campaign advertisements, the SMO may qualify as a
committee with filing obligations additional to the filing obligations of an SMO."

We also noted, however, that the second sentence of this statute does protect the
SMO from the necessity of duplicative reporting. The statute, in full, reads as follows:

File No. A-06-140
Page No. 3

"(c) The production and distribution of slate mailers by a slate
mailer organization shall not be considered making contributions
for purposes of subdivision (b) or (c) of section
82013. If a slate mailer organization makes contributions or
expenditures other than by producing or distributing slate mailers,
and it reports those contributions and expenditures pursuant to
sections 84218 and 84219, no additional campaign reports shall be
required of the slate mailer organization pursuant to section 84200
or 84200.5."

Thus assuming that these SMOs make contributions or expenditures in connection
with advertisements that are not slate mailers, they can fulfill their reporting obligations
under section 84200 and/or 84200.5 by reporting these activities pursuant to sections
84218 and 84219.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

As we explained in the Lacy Advice Letter, No. A-06-017, the Act provides that political ads which do not fit the Act's definition of "slate mailers" do not enjoy the special exemption of section 82048.4(c), so that "when an SMO engages in the production and dissemination of campaign advertisements, the SMO may qualify as a committee with filing obligations additional to the filing obligations of an SMO."
